patches.find: Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question is whether 2026 residuals — (observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage), after factoring out the shadow-coverage term both models share — track the dome's FSF = H(r)/r profile (a function of geographic latitude through dome disc radius) more tightly than they track a magnetic-latitude scaling consistent with Kim & Chang's 30°–50° response zone. Both the dome formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF and a standard-physics prediction ≈ baseline × coverage × f(mag_lat) include coverage as a factor, so coverage is not the discriminator — the real fingerprint is geographic-latitude FSF versus magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat). We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives; if residuals track magnetic-latitude scaling more tightly than FSF, the dome's geographic-latitude scaling is effectively a proxy for the mag-lat Sq response; if FSF beats mag-lat scaling on a clean out-of-sample test, something non-trivial will need explaining.
patches.replace: Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question is whether 2026 residuals — (observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage), under the simplest linear-coverage approximation applied identically to both models — track the dome's FSF = H(r)/r profile (a function of geographic latitude through dome disc radius) more tightly than they track a magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat) profile consistent with Kim & Chang's 30°–50° response zone. To pin the competing prediction down before 2026 data arrives, we will evaluate each of the nine stations at its CGM magnetic latitude (IAGA observatory registry) and use Kim & Chang (2018)'s published 30°–50° Y-component response envelope as a pre-registered reference: stations with magnetic latitude inside the band are predicted to carry an ensemble-median signal, stations outside are predicted to be suppressed — a crude but falsifiable nine-number competitor to the dome's nine FSF numbers. If the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become available we will instead fit a smooth f(mag_lat) across the ensemble and evaluate it per station; either way the standard-model side produces specific per-station predictions, not a vibe. The linear-coverage form common to both predictions is the dome's explicit choice and a first-order approximation for the standard model — a rigorous Sq treatment enters coverage through the ionospheric-conductivity integral with altitude and local-time structure rather than as a bare multiplier — but we adopt it symmetrically so the residual comparison does not bias either direction; any nonlinear-coverage effect will drift both residuals in parallel, leaving the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint intact. We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives; if residuals track the magnetic-latitude prediction more tightly than FSF, the dome's geographic-latitude scaling is effectively a proxy for the mag-lat Sq response; if FSF beats the magnetic-latitude prediction on this pre-registered nine-station test, something non-trivial will need explaining.
patches.rationale: EXP-234: Resolves ISS-1455 (asymmetric test setup — commits to pre-registered mag-lat method per station) and ISS-1456 (linear-coverage approximation now named explicitly and applied symmetrically)
